report.narrative: For sex, 'sex ≠ Male' is predicted 'income' 17% of the time versus 20% for 'sex = Male' (P-rule 84% — within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'sex ≠ Male'; severity is low. For race, 'race ≠ White' is predicted 'income' 16% of the time versus 19% for 'race = White' (P-rule 84% — within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'race ≠ White'; severity is low.

For sex, 'Female' is predicted 'income' 17% of the time versus 20% for 'Male' (P-rule 84%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Female'; severity is low. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 36% to 84%. The actual outcome gap is 19 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 3 pts). For race, 'race ≠ White' is predicted 'income' 16% of the time versus 19% for 'race = White' (P-rule 84%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'race ≠ White'; severity is low. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 61% to 84%. The actual outcome gap is 9 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 3 pts). Accuracy went from 85.6% to 81.7% over 13 iterations.

For Gender, 'Male' is predicted 'legal_entry' 78% of the time versus 94% for 'Female' (P-rule 83%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Male'; severity is severe. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 76% to 83%. The actual outcome gap is 25 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 16 pts). For region_origin, 'Center-West' is predicted 'legal_entry' 72% of the time versus 90% for 'Greater Tunis' (P-rule 80%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Center-West'; severity is severe. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 56% to 80%. The actual outcome gap is 41 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 18 pts). For educ_level, 'Low' is predicted 'legal_entry' 76% of the time versus 95% for 'Higher education' (P-rule 80%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Low'; severity is severe. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 70% to 80%. The actual outcome gap is 26 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 19 pts). Accuracy went from 85.6% to 83.4% over 28 iterations.

For Gender, 'Male' is predicted 'legal_entry' 81% of the time versus 95% for 'Female' (P-rule 85%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Male'; severity is moderate. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 76% to 85%. The actual outcome gap is 25 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 15 pts). For region_origin, 'Center-West' is predicted 'legal_entry' 78% of the time versus 93% for 'Greater Tunis' (P-rule 84%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Center-West'; severity is moderate. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 59% to 84%. The actual outcome gap is 41 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 15 pts). For educ_level, 'Low' is predicted 'legal_entry' 78% of the time versus 95% for 'Higher education' (P-rule 82%, within the 80% bar). The disadvantaged group is 'Low'; severity is severe. Debiasing moved the P-rule from 70% to 82%. The actual outcome gap is 26 pts; the model reduces it (predicted gap 17 pts). Accuracy went from 86.7% to 83.7% over 28 iterations.

The disadvantaged group for 'Gender' is Male, who have a predicted-positive rate 10.6 percentage points lower than Females (72.9% vs 61.5%), and an actual outcome rate 16.7 percentage points lower (73.0% vs 56.3%). The P-rule indicates that the model amplifies the existing gap between genders, as it is higher after debiasing (84.4%) than before (72.7%). For 'region_origin', the disadvantaged group is Center-West, with a predicted-positive rate 12.2 percentage points lower and an actual outcome rate 34.3 percentage points lower compared to Greater Tunis. The P-rule shows that the model amplifies this gap as well, increasing from 54.7% to 81.5%. Debiasing improved the P-rule for both attributes but came at a cost of accuracy, decreasing from 87.9% to 82.5%. It's worth noting that these gaps are not designed into the model but rather flow through correlated proxies, highlighting the need for careful consideration and mitigation of bias in machine learning systems.

The disadvantaged group for each sensitive attribute is as follows: Males have a lower predicted-positive rate compared to females, with a 12.4 percentage point gap (90.5% vs 78.1%) and a P-rule of 0.93; individuals from the Center-West region have a lower actual outcome rate compared to those from Greater Tunis, with a 40 percentage point gap (92.2% vs 52.2%) and a P-rule of 0.53; and individuals with low educational level have a lower predicted-positive rate compared to those with higher education, with a 21.3 percentage point gap (88.7% vs 71.4%) and a P-rule of 0.80.

The model amplifies the actual outcome-rate gaps for all three attributes, as the predicted-positive rates are not aligned with the actual outcomes. Debiasing improved the P-rule from 77.3% to 86.3% for gender, but at the cost of reducing accuracy from 87.3% to 84.0%. The debiasing process also reduced the P-rule for region_origin from 54.0% to 85.5%, but with a corresponding decrease in accuracy. It's worth noting that these gaps are not designed into the model, but rather flow through correlated proxies, highlighting the need for careful consideration of fairness metrics and debiasing techniques.

The classifier's predictions show that the model has made efforts to address biases in its output, particularly for sensitive attributes such as 'Gender' and 'region_origin'. The disadvantaged group for 'Gender' is Male, with a gap of 8.8 percentage points (from 77.3% to 68.5%) or a P-rule ratio of 0.115, indicating that the model has reduced the actual outcome-rate gap. In contrast, the disadvantaged group for 'region_origin' is Center-West, with a gap of 23.9 percentage points (from 54.0% to 30.1%) or a P-rule ratio of 0.442, suggesting that the model amplifies this disparity. The debiasing efforts have improved the P-rule accuracy from 81.5% to 80.5%, but at the cost of reducing overall accuracy from 87.3% to 85.2%. Notably, the gap in actual outcome rates persists despite these changes, indicating that it is not solely a result of biases in the model's predictions, but rather flows through correlated proxies.

The disadvantaged group for each sensitive attribute appears to be males, individuals from the Center-West region, and those with low educational levels. The model's predicted-positive rate gaps between these groups and their respective advantaged counterparts are significant: 12.8 percentage points (or a ratio of 1.28) for Gender, 17.4 percentage points (or a ratio of 1.34) for region_origin, and 12.3 percentage points (or a ratio of 1.23) for educ_level. The model amplifies the actual outcome-rate gaps in these groups, with the exception of females, who have a higher predicted-positive rate than their actual outcome rate. Debiaseding improved the P-rule for Gender by 8.5 percentage points but came at an accuracy cost of 3.5 percentage points. It's worth noting that these disparities are not explicitly designed into the model but rather flow through correlated proxies, highlighting the importance of addressing fairness in machine learning models.

The disadvantaged group for 'Gender' is Male, who have a predicted-positive rate 16.6% lower than Females (80.0% vs 69.4%), resulting in a P-rule of 86.8%. The model amplifies the actual outcome-rate gap between genders, with Females having an actual outcome rate 34.2% higher than Males (98.0% vs 63.8%). For 'region_origin', the disadvantaged group is Center-West, who have a predicted-positive rate 15.3% lower than Greater Tunis (81.8% vs 66.5%), resulting in a P-rule of 81.3%. The model mirrors the actual outcome-rate gap between regions, with Greater Tunis having an actual outcome rate 41.1% higher than Center-West (91.9% vs 50.8%). Debiaseding improved the P-rule from 62.5% to 81.3%, but at a cost of reducing accuracy from 87.8% to 81.2%. The gaps in predicted-positive rates are not designed, but rather flow through correlated proxies such as education level and region of origin.

The classifier's predictions show that the disadvantaged group for 'origin_group' is A, with a predicted-positive rate of 22.1% and an actual outcome rate of 25.5%, resulting in a gap of 3.4 percentage points (P-rule) or approximately 0.69 times the original gap. In contrast, the disadvantaged group for 'background_group' is Y, with a predicted-positive rate of 19.0% and an actual outcome rate of 13.6%, leading to a gap of 5.4 percentage points (P-rule) or about 1.14 times the original gap. The model's debiasing efforts have reduced these gaps, but at the cost of accuracy, with the P-rule improving from 85.8% to 82.5%. However, the actual outcome-rate gap remains unchanged after debiasing, indicating that the model does not amplify or reduce this disparity; it merely adjusts its predictions to minimize the difference between predicted and actual outcomes. The gaps in these sensitive attributes are not designed biases but rather reflect correlations with other proxies that the model has learned from the data.

The disadvantaged group for each sensitive attribute are females, individuals from non-coastal origin zones, and those with lower schooling levels. The model's predicted-positive rate gaps between these groups and their respective advantaged counterparts are 10.7 percentage points (78.2% vs 65.5%) for sex, 6.3 percentage points (71.3% vs 66.8%) for origin zone, and 5.1 percentage points (72.0% vs 66.5%) for schooling. The model amplifies the actual outcome-rate gaps between these groups, with a ratio of 1.64:1 for sex, 1.45:1 for origin zone, and 1.65:1 for schooling. Debiaseding improved the P-rule from 63.3% to 83.7% for sex, but at the cost of decreasing accuracy from 81.9% to 72.0%. Note that these gaps are not designed into the model, but rather flow through correlated proxies such as demographic attributes.

The disadvantaged group for each sensitive attribute are females, individuals from non-coastal origin zones, and those with lower schooling levels. The model's predicted-positive rate gaps between these groups and their respective advantaged counterparts are 8.3 percentage points (56.0% vs 47.7%) for sex, 9.8 percentage points (57.8% vs 48.0%) for origin zone, and 8.3 percentage points (57.9% vs 49.6%) for schooling. The model mirrors the actual outcome-rate gaps between these groups, with females having a significantly higher actual outcome rate than males, individuals from coastal zones having a higher actual outcome rate than those from non-coastal zones, and those with higher schooling levels having a much higher actual outcome rate than those with lower schooling levels. Debiasing improved the P-rule for sex by 35.3 percentage points (from 57.0% to 92.3%), but at the cost of reducing accuracy from 85.7% to 66.5%. The gaps in predicted-positive rates are not designed, but rather flow through correlated proxies such as origin zone and schooling level, which may be related to unobserved factors that influence entry status.
